---
title: "Statistics by Intuition"
subtitle: "A field notebook for bioinformatics — the intuition behind the statistics and machine learning you'll actually use, examples from cutting-edge biology, and rigorous interview prep to make it all stick."
toc: false
---

::: {.field-notes-band}
<div class="fn-eyebrow">Field notebook · statistics & ML for scientists</div>
<div class="fn-title">Understand it first. Compute it second. Defend it in the interview.</div>
<div class="fn-meta">
  <span class="fn-tag">Intuition-first</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Interview-ready</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Interactive figures</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Primary literature</span>
</div>
:::

Most statistics education hands you a formula and hopes the meaning arrives
later. It usually doesn't. This site inverts the order: every topic starts with
a problem a bioinformatician actually has — a suspicious test result, a
single-cell dataset that won't normalize, a batch effect, a model that looks
great on training data and collapses in the wild — and lets the mathematics
emerge from the problem rather than the other way around. The examples are
modern on purpose: single-cell sequencing, neural networks and gradient descent,
and language models applied to biology.

It is built for three readers:

- **Graduate students** learning statistics and bioinformatics, who want
  intuition that outlasts the exam.
- **Late-stage PhDs and postdocs** preparing for hard technical interviews at
  top companies and labs — people who need to explain what a *p*-value actually
  is, choose the right normalization for scRNA-seq, read a residual plot, or
  reason out loud about why a model is overfitting, and who want to have *seen
  the question before*.
- **Working professionals** who need a fast, trustworthy refresher on a key
  concept or workflow before a meeting, an analysis, or a screen.

Throughout, one lens keeps the material honest: **what would a sharp technical
interviewer press you on?** Not trivia. The reasoning: *why this method, what
does it assume, how would you know if it broke, and what would you plot to find
out?*

## How to use this

Each lesson is a small, self-contained unit with the same four parts:

::: {.specimen}
**The anatomy of a lesson**

1. **Lesson plan** — objectives, modules, and the interview questions the
   lesson unlocks.
2. **Background primer** — exactly the prerequisites you need, no more.
3. **In action** — worked examples with numbers you can check by hand, and
   interactive figures where moving a slider *is* the lesson.
4. **Interview problems** — Rosalind-style exercises and the conceptual
   questions an interviewer asks, each with a collapsible model answer.

Read the plan, detour into the primer if anything feels shaky, work the
examples with a pencil, then close the loop on the interview page. The
exercises are where the learning actually happens.
:::

## The one idea to take away

If this site has a single thesis, it's this: **real data doesn't tell you which
method to use — recognizing the *shape* of the problem does.** Is this a
base-rate problem? A maximum-likelihood problem? Does this sequence have memory,
so I should think Markov? Is there no closed form, so I should simulate it? Every
lesson is built to train that reflex, so that when you meet unfamiliar data you
reach for the right family of tools on instinct.

## The curriculum

Five tracks in a deliberate order — reasoning under uncertainty, fitting models
to data, sequence models, machine learning, and finally language and foundation
models. Each leans on the one before it. See the [full roadmap](roadmap.qmd) for
every planned lesson and how they connect.

::: {.track-grid}
<a class="track-card track-a" href="roadmap.qmd#track-a">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track A</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Reasoning under uncertainty</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Bayes, <em>p</em>-values, <em>t</em>-tests, confidence
  intervals, Monte Carlo simulation, multiple testing, power.</p>
  <span class="tc-status">Lesson 1 live</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-b" href="roadmap.qmd#track-b">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track B</div>
  <div class="tc-title">From data to models</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Normalization, linear regression, maximum likelihood,
  gradient descent, GLMs, and regularization.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-c" href="roadmap.qmd#track-c">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track C</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Modeling sequences</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Markov chains and hidden Markov models — data with memory:
  gene finding, profile HMMs, Viterbi, base-calling.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-d" href="roadmap.qmd#track-d">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track D</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Machine learning</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">The ML workflow as flowcharts, model evaluation,
  dimensionality reduction, and neural networks.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
<a class="track-card track-e" href="roadmap.qmd#track-e">
  <div class="tc-eyebrow">Track E</div>
  <div class="tc-title">Language &amp; foundation models</div>
  <p class="tc-desc">Embeddings, what a language model learns, and foundation
  models for biological data — the destination.</p>
  <span class="tc-status planned">Outlined</span>
</a>
:::

## Start here

If you're new, begin with **[Lesson 1 — Bayesian Statistics](lessons/01-bayesian-statistics/index.qmd)**.
It teaches the single most useful habit in all of applied statistics — *never
evaluate evidence without asking how common the thing is* — and everything in
the inference track builds on it.

::: {.lens}
Bayesian reasoning is not a niche topic you can skip. Base rates govern why a
"significant" result can still probably be false [@ioannidis2005], why a
sensitive diagnostic can mislead, and why a classifier's precision cratered
when you deployed it on rare events. Interviewers return to it constantly
because it exposes whether you *reason* about uncertainty or merely compute
with it.
:::
